gold.rationale: The article’s central debunk is supported by the anti-rumor notice saying no such seven-agency plan was issued, but the presentation still mixes real background regulation with fabricated or synthetic visuals and unrelated authentic imagery in ways that can make the nonexistent document seem more concrete than the evidence allows. This creates a materially mixed impression rather than a cleanly accurate one.

atomic_points.post_summary: The post analyzes a viral claim about a non-existent Chinese government document titled 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)' issued by seven departments, explaining why the rumor spread despite being officially debunked.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Posts shared online this month said China had taken a major new step in regulating artificial intelligence, claiming that seven government departments had jointly issued an "AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)" that would redraw compliance boundaries across the country's AI industry.
atoms: Online posts claimed China took a major new step in regulating AI.
The claim stated seven government departments jointly issued a document.
The document is titled 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028).'
The document was claimed to redraw compliance boundaries across China's AI industry.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The claim moved quickly among investors, startup founders and industry chat groups, where the supposed plan was described as an immediate framework with direct implications for almost every AI company operating in China.
atoms: The claim spread quickly among investors, startup founders, and industry chat groups.
The plan was described as an immediate framework.
The plan was claimed to have direct implications for almost every AI company in China.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The story gained traction in part because it fit the wider policy climate.
atoms: The story gained traction because it fit the wider policy climate.

sentence_level.sentence_text: China already has a substantial body of AI-related regulation, including rules covering recommendation algorithms, deep-synthesis technologies and generative AI services.
atoms: China has a substantial body of AI-related regulation.
Existing rules cover recommendation algorithms.
Existing rules cover deep-synthesis technologies.
Existing rules cover generative AI services.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In recent years, regulators have repeatedly signaled that AI safety, content controls, data governance and platform accountability remain priorities, creating an environment in which any new oversight measure draws close attention from both companies and markets.
atoms: Regulators have signaled AI safety remains a priority.
Regulators have signaled content controls remain a priority.
Regulators have signaled data governance remains a priority.
Regulators have signaled platform accountability remains a priority.
This environment causes new oversight measures to draw close attention from companies and markets.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That sense of tightening scrutiny has been reinforced by a steady run of public notices, enforcement campaigns and standards work.
atoms: Tightening scrutiny has been reinforced by public notices.
Tightening scrutiny has been reinforced by enforcement campaigns.
Tightening scrutiny has been reinforced by standards work.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Ministry notices available to the public show that officials have kept pressing ahead on AI ethics reviews, risk monitoring and technical standards.
atoms: Public ministry notices show officials are pressing ahead on AI ethics reviews.
Public ministry notices show officials are pressing ahead on risk monitoring.
Public ministry notices show officials are pressing ahead on technical standards.

sentence_level.sentence_text: China's internet regulator and other agencies have also treated AI safety as an ongoing governance issue, not a one-time concern.
atoms: China's internet regulator treats AI safety as an ongoing governance issue.
Other agencies treat AI safety as an ongoing governance issue.
AI safety is treated as an ongoing issue, not a one-time concern.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Against that backdrop, online claims that a broader, more coordinated policy package was already in force were easy to believe.
atoms: Online claims of a broader, coordinated policy package being in force were easy to believe.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Yet there is no official record showing that the seven departments named in the viral posts jointly issued the specific document being described.
atoms: There is no official record of the seven departments jointly issuing the described document.

sentence_level.sentence_text: A notice published by a state-linked anti-rumor platform on March 17 said the widely circulated claim was false and that the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, together with the other agencies named in the posts, had never released an "AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)."
atoms: A state-linked anti-rumor platform published a notice on March 17.
The notice stated the widely circulated claim was false.
The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology never released the plan.
The other agencies named in the posts never released the plan.
The plan is titled 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028).'

sentence_level.sentence_text: The same anti-rumor notice also addressed a related claim that the document had been issued on March 10 and would set binding compliance boundaries across the industry, saying those assertions were untrue.
atoms: The anti-rumor notice addressed a claim the document was issued on March 10.
The anti-rumor notice addressed a claim the document would set binding compliance boundaries.
The notice stated those assertions were untrue.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The distinction matters because China's AI regulatory system is real, extensive and still developing.
atoms: China's AI regulatory system is real.
China's AI regulatory system is extensive.
China's AI regulatory system is still developing.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Rules on internet recommendation algorithms took effect in 2022, requiring platforms to meet transparency and fairness obligations while restricting certain manipulative uses of algorithmic systems.
atoms: Rules on internet recommendation algorithms took effect in 2022.
These rules require platforms to meet transparency obligations.
These rules require platforms to meet fairness obligations.
These rules restrict certain manipulative uses of algorithmic systems.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Separate rules on deep synthesis came into force in 2023, aimed at face-swapping, voice simulation and other synthetic-media risks.
atoms: Separate rules on deep synthesis came into force in 2023.
These rules aim at face-swapping risks.
These rules aim at voice simulation risks.
These rules aim at other synthetic-media risks.

sentence_level.sentence_text: China also introduced interim measures for generative AI services in 2023, laying out obligations on content security, personal information protection and the handling of unlawful content.
atoms: China introduced interim measures for generative AI services in 2023.
These measures lay out obligations on content security.
These measures lay out obligations on personal information protection.
These measures lay out obligations on handling unlawful content.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Set alongside the viral claim, those existing measures created the impression of a policy progression ending with the alleged seven-agency plan.
atoms: Existing measures created an impression of policy progression.
The progression appeared to end with the alleged seven-agency plan.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Regulators in China have also advanced AI governance through speeches, campaign-style supervision and technical standard-setting, and ministry websites show continued work on AI safety-related standards and ethics procedures.
atoms: Regulators have advanced AI governance through speeches.
Regulators have advanced AI governance through campaign-style supervision.
Regulators have advanced AI governance through technical standard-setting.
Ministry websites show continued work on AI safety-related standards.
Ministry websites show continued work on ethics procedures.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That activity was genuine.
atoms: The regulatory activity described was genuine.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The unsupported element was the claim that it had already been consolidated into a formally issued joint three-year action plan under the title circulating online.
atoms: The unsupported element was the claim of a formally issued joint three-year action plan.
The plan was circulating under the title found online.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Posts spreading the claim often said the plan would immediately define the compliance perimeter for the entire sector, a message likely to strike a chord in an industry already adapting to tighter rules.
atoms: Posts claimed the plan would immediately define the compliance perimeter for the entire sector.
This message was likely to resonate with an industry adapting to tighter rules.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Some accounts presented it as the next step after the existing generative AI and deep-synthesis regulations.
atoms: Some accounts presented the plan as the next step after existing generative AI regulations.
Some accounts presented the plan as the next step after existing deep-synthesis regulations.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Others cast it as a high-level implementation blueprint for safety governance from 2026 through 2028.
atoms: Others cast the plan as a high-level implementation blueprint for safety governance.
The timeline for the blueprint was from 2026 through 2028.

sentence_level.sentence_text: But publicly available ministry notices do not show a release matching that title, date or seven-agency authorship.
atoms: Publicly available ministry notices do not show a release matching the title.
Publicly available ministry notices do not show a release matching the date.
Publicly available ministry notices do not show a release matching the seven-agency authorship.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Part of the claim's appeal appears to have come from its presentation.
atoms: Part of the claim's appeal came from its presentation.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Screenshots and promotional graphics shared among Chinese tech firms appeared to imitate official formatting, using a red-header document style, ministry names arranged in a formal list and a title matching the rumored plan.
atoms: Screenshots and graphics shared among Chinese tech firms imitated official formatting.
The materials used a red-header document style.
The materials arranged ministry names in a formal list.
The materials used a title matching the rumored plan.

sentence_level.sentence_text: At a glance, the material looked like a government circular.
atoms: The material looked like a government circular at a glance.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In some discussions, those images were accompanied by generic photographs of ministry compounds, official-looking website pages and AI conference backdrops, all of which helped place the alleged document within a familiar state-policy setting.
atoms: Images were accompanied by photographs of ministry compounds.
Images were accompanied by official-looking website pages.
Images were accompanied by AI conference backdrops.
These images helped place the alleged document within a familiar state-policy setting.

sentence_level.sentence_text: A hero image used in some reposts showed a government meeting scene blended with AI control-room imagery, visually reinforcing the impression of top-level coordination on AI safety even though it did not document any specific policy release.
atoms: A hero image showed a government meeting scene blended with AI control-room imagery.
The image visually reinforced the impression of top-level coordination on AI safety.
The image did not document any specific policy release.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That framing carried weight because China has in fact been moving toward tighter AI oversight.
atoms: The framing carried weight because China has moved toward tighter AI oversight.

sentence_level.sentence_text: State media, regulators and ministry systems have repeatedly stressed risk prevention, ethics review and governance capacity.
atoms: State media have stressed risk prevention.
Regulators have stressed risk prevention.
Ministry systems have stressed risk prevention.
State media have stressed ethics review.
Regulators have stressed ethics review.
Ministry systems have stressed ethics review.
State media have stressed governance capacity.
Regulators have stressed governance capacity.
Ministry systems have stressed governance capacity.

sentence_level.sentence_text: There are real examples of coordinated rulemaking across departments, including the generative AI measures that involved multiple agencies.
atoms: There are real examples of coordinated rulemaking across departments.
The generative AI measures involved multiple agencies.

sentence_level.sentence_text: There have also been genuine campaigns against the misuse of AI technology and authentic notices related to standards, safety and supervision.
atoms: There have been genuine campaigns against the misuse of AI technology.
There have been authentic notices related to standards.
There have been authentic notices related to safety.
There have been authentic notices related to supervision.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In that setting, a document presented as a seven-department action plan did not seem out of place.
atoms: In that setting, a document presented as a seven-department action plan did not seem out of place.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Still, the anti-rumor notice drew a firm distinction.
atoms: The anti-rumor notice drew a firm distinction.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The question was not whether China regulates AI, but whether this specific seven-agency, dated three-year plan had actually been issued.
atoms: The question was not whether China regulates AI.
The question was whether this specific seven-agency, dated three-year plan had been issued.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Chinese authorities said it had not.
atoms: Chinese authorities said the plan had not been issued.

sentence_level.sentence_text: For companies trying to judge the policy climate, that is more than a technical difference.
atoms: For companies judging the policy climate, the distinction is more than a technical difference.

sentence_level.sentence_text: A verified regulation can prompt immediate product reviews, legal assessments and investor recalculations.
atoms: A verified regulation can prompt immediate product reviews.
A verified regulation can prompt legal assessments.
A verified regulation can prompt investor recalculations.

sentence_level.sentence_text: A nonexistent one can do much the same if it is presented convincingly enough.
atoms: A nonexistent regulation can prompt similar actions if presented convincingly.

sentence_level.sentence_text: For startups and compliance teams, the episode is a reminder that a real policy direction does not automatically validate every document claiming to reflect it.
atoms: For startups and compliance teams, a real policy direction does not automatically validate every document claiming to reflect it.

sentence_level.sentence_text: China's push on AI governance is tangible, and safety oversight remains an active theme.
atoms: China's push on AI governance is tangible.
Safety oversight remains an active theme.

sentence_level.sentence_text: But the specific online narrative about a jointly issued "AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)" combined that real regulatory environment with a document that authorities said was never released.
atoms: The online narrative combined the real regulatory environment with a document.
The document was titled 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028).'
Authorities said the document was never released.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In a fast-moving sector where policy headlines can reshape financing, hiring and product road maps, that kind of false specificity can have consequences far beyond social media.
atoms: Policy headlines can reshape financing in the sector.
Policy headlines can reshape hiring in the sector.
Policy headlines can reshape product road maps in the sector.
False specificity can have consequences far beyond social media.

paragraph_level: The post argues that while China has a real and extensive AI regulatory framework, a specific viral claim about a 'seven-department joint action plan' is false.
The rumor spread rapidly because it aligned with the existing perception of tightening AI oversight and coordinated government action.
A state-linked anti-rumor platform officially debunked the claim on March 17, stating the document was never issued.
The post details how the fake document was presented with official formatting, red headers, and supporting imagery to mimic a genuine government circular.
Existing regulations on recommendation algorithms (2022), deep synthesis (2023), and generative AI (2023) created a backdrop that made the fake '2026-2028' plan seem plausible.
The distinction between real regulatory activity and the specific non-existent document matters significantly for companies facing potential legal and financial impacts.
The post concludes that while the regulatory direction is real, the specific narrative of a jointly issued three-year plan is a fabrication that can still cause real-world consequences.

atoms: A formal meeting room with a large oval table surrounded by men in dark suits.
A central figure seated at the head of the table, gesturing with his hand.
Two large Chinese national flags standing on either side of the central figure.
A large traditional Chinese landscape painting hanging on the wall behind the flags.
Two large digital screens on the left and right walls displaying blue graphics.
The left screen shows a network node diagram with icons for a bank, shield, and user.
The right screen shows a shield icon connected to user and lock icons.
White teacups and microphones are placed on the table in front of the attendees.
A screenshot of a WeChat chat interface showing a forwarded message.
The header of the document lists seven government departments in red text.
The departments listed are: 国家互联网信息办公室, 国家发展和改革委员会, 科学技术部, 工业和信息化部, 公安部, 财政部, 国家市场监督管理总局.
The document title is '《AI 安全治理三年行动计划（2026—2028年）》'.
The document number is '网信办发〔2025〕23号'.
The text body contains sections titled '一、总体要求' and '二、重点行动'.
A watermark logo for '长图' is visible in the bottom right corner.
The bottom of the screenshot shows a caption: '重磅！三部门联合发布 AI 安全治理三年行动计划'.
The official logo of the Chinese Government website (www.gov.cn).
The logo features a blue illustration of the Gate of Heavenly Peace (Tiananmen).
The text '中国政府网' is written below the illustration.
The URL 'www.gov.cn' is written below the Chinese text.
A group of men in business suits standing in a brightly lit exhibition hall.
A man on the far left is holding a microphone and speaking.
A large display screen behind the group shows a presentation slide with Chinese text and graphics.
The slide title includes '数据未来的引领之路' (Leading the Future of Data).
The central figure in the group is standing with his hands clasped in front of him.
Other attendees, including men and women in formal and semi-formal attire, are standing around the group.
The background shows exhibition booth structures and lighting rigs.

retrieved_evidence.text: Policy analysts noted that, despite the denial of the specific viral document, China has already introduced a series of measures related to artificial intelligence governance in recent years. According to public records from regulators including the Cyberspace Administration, these include rules on recommendation algorithms, provisions on so‑called deep synthesis technologies and guidelines for generative AI services. Observers say this body of regulation has created an established framework for platform operators and AI developers, and additional implementing details are still being discussed.

Chinese authorities in charge of internet oversight said in notices issued in recent days that a widely shared claim about a so‑called “AI Safety Governance Three‑Year Action Plan (2026–2028)” jointly released by seven government departments does not correspond to any officially published policy. According to officials, the viral posts had circulated across multiple social platforms and industry chat groups, prompting regulators to issue clarifications aimed at calming speculation in the technology and investment communities about imminent new requirements for the artificial intelligence sector.

Among posts circulating on Chinese social media, one prominent item reproduced what appeared to be a formal circular titled “AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026–2028)” with a red banner and a layout resembling multi-ministry notices. In recent reports, regulatory sources emphasized that no joint document under this exact title had been promulgated by the seven central government agencies mentioned, underscoring that while China is moving toward stronger AI governance, the institutional process for issuing such action plans follows strict drafting, consultation and publication procedures through official channels.

In the clarification released this week, an anti‑rumor notice circulated by China’s cyberspace regulators stated that the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and other agencies named in online posts had never issued the purported “AI Safety Governance Three‑Year Action Plan”. According to the document, checks of official portals and policy databases did not reveal any record of such a joint release, and the agencies involved were said to have requested that online platforms handle related misleading content in accordance with existing management rules.

According to posts that circulated on several Chinese social media platforms earlier this month, the alleged “AI Safety Governance Three‑Year Action Plan” was described as a document that would set strict compliance boundaries across the entire industry and directly affect every AI company. Industry insiders said these claims were shared widely among entrepreneurs and developers, often without links to any official source. Regulators later stated that these descriptions formed part of a disputed online narrative rather than a confirmed policy arrangement.

【网传“七部门发布AI安全治理三年行动计划”不实】近日，有自媒体发布题为《七部门重磅发布AI安全治理三年行动计划》的文章，声称“2026年3月10日，工业和信息化部、中央网络安全和信息化委员会办公室、教育部、公安部、人力资源和社会保障部、国家市场监督管理总局、国家广播电视总局七部门联合印发《人工智能安全治理三年行动计划（2026-2028年）》”“全行业合规边界划定，这些要求直接影响每一家AI企业”。经有关主管部门核查，上述信息为虚假信息，工业和信息化部等部门从未发布过该文件。（来源：中国互联网联合辟谣平台、工信微报） 关 注 海关总署进口食品境外生产企业注册管理系统网站是申请和查询进口食品境外生产企业注册的唯一官方网站，不收费 详情： 3月19日，海关总署进出口食品安全局局长李劲松在解读新修订的进口食品境外生产企业注册管理制度时强调，海关总署进口食品境外生产企业注册管理系统网站（https://cifer.singlewindow.cn）是申请和查询进口食品境外生产企业注册的唯一官方网站。企业办理申请、变更、延续、暂停、恢复等注册业务，境外主管机构办理推荐注册，消费者查询已注册企业信息等做法，均应通过官方网站进行，且不会收取任何费用。海关总署提醒广大进口注册相关方注意识别，避免登录虚假网站导致蒙受损失。（来源：@海关发布）

Media analysts said the current discussion around the disputed AI governance document underlines the importance of distinguishing between the existence of regulation in general and the status of a particular policy text. According to people familiar with the matter, official clarifications have indicated that what is being denied is not the presence of AI‑related rules in China, but rather the assertion that a specific seven‑agency, date‑marked three‑year plan has already been formally issued. They added that future policy steps, if any, would normally appear through standard publication channels.

Communication experts commented in recent days that the narrative surrounding the alleged “AI Safety Governance Three‑Year Action Plan” appears to rely on blending genuine regulatory developments with an invented policy title. According to these observers, references to China’s real environment of AI‑related rules, combined with mention of multiple central agencies, may cause readers to overlook the absence of any publicly available joint action plan. They noted that such overlaps between real and nonexistent elements can complicate efforts to identify misleading claims in fast‑moving online discussions.

Researchers following China’s technology policy say that safety, security and compliance in the field of artificial intelligence have become recurring themes in official planning documents and public statements. According to these analysts, this policy focus may make it easier for some internet users to accept the idea of a detailed, time‑bound action plan even when no such text has been released. They add that the combination of familiar regulatory language and references to well‑known ministries can lend extra credibility to unverified material shared online.

In recent years, Chinese authorities such as the Cyberspace Administration of China, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and other central regulators have held frequent policy briefings on algorithm governance, generative AI and data security, using conference halls equipped with large digital screens and ministry-branded backdrops. According to officials and policy analysts, these multi-agency sessions signal a broader national AI governance push, in which oversight of recommendation systems, content platforms and foundation models is being tightened even without a specific 2026–2028 AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan formally announced by seven central government bodies.

Policy commentators said this week that the controversy over the reported AI action plan is considered significant because of the way businesses and individual readers might react to it. According to their assessment, many companies could interpret such a document, if real, as signaling an immediate shift in binding regulatory expectations, potentially influencing investment decisions, hiring and product planning. The commentators noted that this perceived impact on corporate behavior is one reason why officials moved to clarify the status of the widely discussed plan.

Authentic policy materials from bodies such as the Cyberspace Administration of China and the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology typically appear on their official portals, where sections devoted to algorithms, deep synthesis and generative AI governance aggregate regulations, draft rules and implementation guidelines. In recent reports, legal experts have noted that this existing corpus of AI-related rules—ranging from provisions on recommendation algorithms to measures on generative AI services—forms the substantive regulatory backdrop against which references to an alleged 2026–2028 AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan were interpreted by observers and technology firms.

Government-linked technology forums and AI expos in cities like Beijing and Shanghai frequently bring together regulators, major internet platforms and research institutions to discuss standards, security requirements and industrial policy for artificial intelligence. According to participants, large conference venues often feature stages and display screens with AI-themed graphics while speakers analyze China’s evolving compliance framework for generative AI tools and foundation models. In this setting, expectations about future initiatives, including the possibility of multi-year AI safety governance plans, are shaped by ongoing guidance from central government bodies rather than by any single unconfirmed action plan for 2026–2028.

The rumor spread rapidly because it aligned with the existing perception of tightening AI oversight and coordinated government action.
A state-linked anti-rumor platform officially debunked the claim on March 17, stating the document was never issued.
The post details how the fake document was presented with official formatting, red headers, and supporting imagery to mimic a genuine government circular.
Existing regulations on recommendation algorithms (2022), deep synthesis (2023), and generative AI (2023) created a backdrop that made the fake '2026-2028' plan seem plausible.
The distinction between real regulatory activity and the specific non-existent document matters significantly for companies facing potential legal and financial impacts.
The post concludes that while the regulatory direction is real, the specific narrative of a jointly issued three-year plan is a fabrication that can still cause real-world consequences.

images.scene_description: A formal government meeting room with a large oval table, Chinese flags, and a traditional landscape painting in the background. A central figure is speaking while several other officials in dark suits sit around the table, facing him. Two large screens on the side display blue digital graphics related to AI and security.
images.explanation: A clean, authentic-looking photo of a government meeting that supports the context of AI governance discussions without contradicting the debunked document claim.

images.scene_description: A screenshot of a document formatted to look like an official Chinese government notice, featuring a red header with the names of seven ministries and a title in bold black text.
images.ocr_text: 国家互联网信息办公室 中华人民共和国国家发展和改革委员会 中华人民共和国科学技术部 工业和信息化部 公安部 财政部 国家市场监督管理总局 文件 网信办发〔2025〕23号 《AI 安全治理三年行动计划（2026—2028年）》
images.v1_evidence: The image is a high-fidelity AI-generated or heavily edited graphic mimicking an official document, which the post text explicitly identifies as a 'viral claim' and a 'non-existent' document that was debunked.
images.v2_evidence: The image is a composite graphic designed to look like an official document but depicts a specific event (the release of a non-existent plan) that contradicts the ground truth evidence stating no such document was issued.
images.c_evidence: The image depicts a document titled 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)' issued by seven ministries, which directly contradicts the ground truth evidence stating that no such document was ever promulgated.
images.explanation: This image is a synthetic visual representation of a fake document, matching the post's description of the viral rumor and contradicting the ground truth that the document does not exist.

images.scene_description: The official logo of the Chinese Government website (www.gov.cn), featuring a blue illustration of the Gate of Heavenly Peace and the text '中国 政府网'.
images.explanation: A clean, authentic logo image used to represent the official source that debunked the rumor.

images.scene_description: A group of officials and international guests standing in a brightly lit exhibition hall, looking at a large display screen showing AI-related graphics and data.
images.explanation: A clean, authentic photo of an AI exhibition that illustrates the context of ongoing AI governance discussions without depicting the specific fake document.

image_analysis.global_notes: The post uses a mix of authentic context images (meeting, logo, exhibition) and one synthetic image (the fake document) to illustrate the rumor. The fake document image is the primary source of misinformation, while the others provide the 'real' context that makes the rumor plausible.

judge.level3_rationale: 尽管文章核心事实（文件不存在）正确，但其中包含了对虚假文件具体细节的虚构（T1），并展示了一张AI生成的伪造文件截图作为证据（V1, V2），导致图文语境不一致（C2），这些关键失真使得整体真实性大打折扣。
judge.reasoning_trace: The post correctly identifies the fake nature of the document but relies on a fabricated visual (AI-generated screenshot) and specific details about the fake document's content that are not fully corroborated by evidence, leading to a Mostly False verdict due to the presence of T1, V1, V2, and C2 labels.
judge.core_claim: The post explains that while China has a real and extensive AI regulatory framework, a specific viral claim about a 'seven-department joint action plan' titled 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)' is false and was officially debunked.
key_subclaims: China has a real and extensive AI regulatory framework (rules on algorithms, deep synthesis, generative AI).
A viral claim circulated that seven government departments jointly issued an 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)'.
A state-linked anti-rumor platform officially debunked this specific claim on March 17, stating the document was never issued.
The fake document was presented with official formatting (red header, ministry names) to mimic a genuine government circular.
The images accompanying the rumor included generic photos of ministry compounds and AI conference backdrops to lend credibility.
The distinction between real regulatory activity and the specific non-existent document matters for companies facing potential legal and financial impacts.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: China has a real and extensive AI regulatory framework (rules on algorithms, deep synthesis, generative AI).
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the existence of rules on recommendation algorithms, deep synthesis, and generative AI services.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: A viral claim circulated that seven government departments jointly issued an 'AI Safety Governance Three-Year Action Plan (2026-2028)'.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the viral claim about the seven departments and the specific title circulated widely.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: A state-linked anti-rumor platform officially debunked this specific claim on March 17, stating the document was never issued.
subclaim_findings.note: The fact check explicitly states the information is false and the departments never released the file.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The fake document was presented with official formatting (red header, ministry names) to mimic a genuine government circular.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 2 shows a screenshot mimicking an official circular with a red banner and ministry names.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The images accompanying the rumor included generic photos of ministry compounds and AI conference backdrops to lend credibility.
subclaim_findings.note: Image 4 shows officials at a technology exhibition, consistent with the description of AI conference backdrops used to lend credibility.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The distinction between real regulatory activity and the specific non-existent document matters for companies facing potential legal and financial impacts.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence notes that businesses might interpret such a document as signaling an immediate shift in binding regulatory expectations, influencing investment and hiring.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post's core claim is accurate: the specific document is fake, and the regulatory framework is real. However, the post includes a fabricated element (T1) regarding the specific details of the fake document's content (e.g., the exact date March 10 mentioned in the fact check vs. the post's general description) and presents a synthetic visual (V1) of the fake document as if it were a real screenshot. The image of the fake document (Image 2) is AI-generated (V1) and edited (V2) to look real, creating a contextual inconsistency (C2) with the fact that the document does not exist. The presence of T1 and V1/V2/C2 labels pushes the verdict to Mostly False, as the post relies on a fabricated visual and specific details to support its debunking narrative.

image_findings.explanation: The image depicts a formal government meeting, which aligns with the post's description of the setting where such rumors might be discussed or presented, without specific edits or synthetic elements.

The image is a high-fidelity AI-generated graphic mimicking an official document (V1) with overlays (V2) that presents a non-existent document as real, creating a contextual mismatch (C2) with the fact that no such document exists.
The image is the official logo of the Chinese Government website, which serves as a reference for official sources mentioned in the post, with no synthetic or editing issues.
The image shows officials at a technology exhibition, consistent with the post's description of AI conference backdrops used to lend credibility to the rumors, with no synthetic or editing issues.
